item.title: Last day to ride the Säntis cable car
item.description: From May 1st, the Säntis cable car will be closed for renovations. The new cabins will be able to transport up to 85 passengers.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Last day to ride the Säntis cable car: April 30, 2026
- Total renovation of the cable car with a cost of 30 million francs
- New cabins with a capacity of 85 passengers

## Key facts
- **What**: Total renovation of the Säntis cable car
- When: From May 1, 2026
- Where: Säntis, Eastern Switzerland
- Who: Säntis cable car authorities
- Amount: 30 million francs

The Säntis cable car, one of the most frequented ski lifts in Switzerland, is making its last passenger transport today, April 30. From tomorrow, May 1, and until late autumn, the facility will be completely renovated. The new cabins will be able to transport up to 85 passengers, maintaining a journey time of approximately eight minutes. The total cost of the renovation is expected to be 30 million francs.

During the construction period, the Säntis will only be reachable on foot and the restaurant at the top will remain closed. The new cabins will offer better comfort and greater stability in case of strong gusts of wind. The actual opening date of the new cable car will depend on the weather conditions of the coming months.

### Implications for visitors
Visitors planning to go up to the Säntis will have to consider the absence of the cable car for several months. Those who had booked events at the top will not be able to use the facilities until the end of the year. The mountain, which is 2,502 meters high, is a popular destination for hikers and tourists, but during the renovation work, access will be limited.

### History of the cable car
Put into operation in the summer of 1935, the Säntis cable car has a long history of service. The facilities were completely modernized for the last time in 1974, while in 2000 new cabins were introduced. With around half a million passengers per year, the cable car is a pillar of tourism in Eastern Switzerland.

### What to do if you want to visit the Säntis
For those who still want to visit the Säntis during the closure period, the main option remains the hike. However, it is important to be prepared for a physically demanding undertaking, given the altitude and variable weather conditions.

### Future prospects
Once the work is completed, the new cable car promises to offer a more comfortable and safe travel experience. The authorities hope to reopen the facility in late autumn, but the exact date will depend on the weather conditions. Visitors are invited to monitor official updates to find out the exact reopening dates.

### Conclusion
The renovation of the Säntis cable car represents an important step to improve the tourist infrastructure of the region. Although the temporary closure may cause inconvenience, the long-term benefits for visitors and local operators will be significant. It is advisable to plan visits taking into account this closure and to explore the available alternatives to enjoy the beauty of the Säntis even during the works.

For more information and to plan your visit, consult the [travel calculator](nav:exchange). - ## Analysis of the implications
The renovation of the Säntis cable car will have a significant impact on tourism in eastern Switzerland. The closure of the facility for several months could influence the flow of visitors, particularly during the summer season, when the Säntis is a very popular destination.

### Impact on tourism
The Säntis cable car is one of the main tourist attractions in the region. Its closure could lead to a reduction in the number of visitors, with consequent repercussions for local businesses, such as restaurants, shops, and tourist guides. However, the reopening with new cabins and technical improvements could attract a greater number of tourists once the work is completed.

### Alternatives for visitors
During the closure period, visitors can consider other attractions in the region. For example, the nearby town of Appenzell offers numerous opportunities for excursions and outdoor activities. Additionally, other cable cars and ski lifts in the Swiss Alps can offer similar experiences.

### Preparation for reopening
The cable car authorities are working to ensure that the reopening takes place as promptly as possible. However, it is important that visitors are aware of possible variations in the completion times of the work. Monitoring official updates and planning visits accordingly will be fundamental.

### Long-term benefits
Despite the temporary inconveniences, the improvements made to the Säntis cable car will bring numerous long-term benefits. The new cabins will offer greater comfort and safety, improving the overall passenger experience. Furthermore, the updated infrastructure could attract an even greater number of visitors, contributing to the growth of tourism in the region.

### Conclusion
The renovation of the Säntis cable car is an important investment for the future of tourism in eastern Switzerland. Although the temporary closure may pose a challenge, the long-term benefits will be significant. Visitors are invited to plan their visits taking into account this closure and to explore the available alternatives to enjoy the beauty of the Swiss Alps even during the works.

For more information and to plan your visit, consult the [travel calculator](nav:exchange).

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do during the closure
During the closure of the Säntis cable car, there are several options for visitors who still wish to explore the region. Here are some suggestions to make the most of your visit.

### Hiking
If you are a hiking enthusiast, the Säntis offers numerous trails that can be explored on foot. However, it is important to be prepared for a physically demanding endeavor, given the altitude and variable weather conditions. Make sure you have the appropriate equipment and inform yourself about the trail conditions before setting out.

### Visiting other attractions
Eastern Switzerland offers numerous other attractions that can be visited during the closure of the Säntis cable car. For example, the nearby town of Appenzell is famous for its picturesque landscapes and local traditions. Additionally, other cable cars and ski lifts in the Swiss Alps can offer similar experiences.

### Planning your visit
If you are planning a visit to the Säntis, it is important to take into account the closure of the cable car. Monitor official updates to know the exact reopening dates and plan your visit accordingly. Also consider exploring other attractions in the region to make the most of your experience.

### Conclusion
The renovation of the Säntis cable car represents an opportunity to improve the tourist infrastructure of the region. Although the temporary closure may cause inconvenience, the long-term benefits for visitors and local operators will be significant. Plan your visit taking into account this closure and explore the available alternatives to enjoy the beauty of the Swiss Alps even during the works.

For more information and to plan your visit, consult the [travel calculator](nav:exchange).

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/svizzera/svizzera/1922082/ultimo-giorno-utile-per-salire-sul-santis-in-funivia)*

item.title: Nightmare A2 return: queues from Rivera to Lugano Sud
item.description: Black day for commuters: accidents and heavy traffic on the A2, queues from Rivera to Lugano Sud and border crossings congested
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Traffic jam on the A2 from Rivera to Lugano Sud
- Accidents in Grancia and San Salvatore tunnel
- Border crossings congested

## Key facts
- **What**: Heavy traffic and accidents on the A2
- When: April 30, 2026
- Where: A2, Grancia area, San Salvatore tunnel, border crossings
- Who: Commuters and drivers
- Impact: Traffic jams from Rivera to Lugano Sud

A nightmarish day of car commuting this Thursday. The exodus for the long weekend and two accidents in the Grancia area played a role. A painful (and quite predictable) morning rush hour was followed by a return journey on the A2 with severe traffic congestion. The long weekend (with the exodus southward, also from our canton), rush hour traffic, and an accident - which occurred just before 5 PM - near the San Salvatore tunnel all contributed. Following the incident, the overtaking lane was closed. According to reports, a second accident also occurred, a rear-end collision involving a truck and a car near the Lugano Sud exit. At the moment, traffic jams heading south start already near Rivera. There are also traffic jams near the border crossings with the customs offices of Ponte Tresa, Chiasso, and Stabio, which are particularly congested. Slowdowns are also occurring at Brusata.

### Implications for cross-border workers
Cross-border workers who commute to Switzerland and reside in Italy are among the most affected by this situation. Long traffic jams and slowdowns can result in significant delays in returning home, with consequent impacts on quality of life and leisure time. Additionally, fuel costs and vehicle wear and tear increase with the kilometers traveled in heavy traffic conditions.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Compared to periods of normal traffic, today's situation is definitely worse. Traffic jams starting already from Rivera and extending to Lugano Sud are a clear sign of exceptional traffic. Even the border crossings, such as Ponte Tresa, Chiasso, and Stabio, are particularly congested, which can cause further delays for those who need to cross the border.

### Possible scenarios
If the situation does not improve, commuters may need to consider alternatives to driving, such as using public transportation or carpooling. However, public transportation may also be subject to delays and overcrowding due to heavy traffic.

### Conclusion
Today's situation is an example of how heavy traffic and accidents can turn normal commuting into a nightmare. For cross-border workers, in particular, this situation represents an additional challenge that can negatively influence their quality of life and well-being. - ## Analysis of the causes of traffic
The current situation is the result of a combination of factors. First, the long weekend has led to an exodus southwards, with many residents of the Canton of Ticino heading to Italy for the bank holiday. This has caused a significant increase in traffic on the A2, especially in the southbound direction. Furthermore, accidents that occurred in the Grancia area and near the San Salvatore tunnel have contributed to worsening the situation, causing the closure of the overtaking lane and significant slowdowns.

### Impact on border crossings
The border crossings at Ponte Tresa, Chiasso, and Stabio are particularly congested. This may be due not only to heavy traffic but also to more stringent customs checks or technical issues. Delays at these crossings can cause queues that extend for several kilometers, further increasing travel times for commuters.

### Comparison with other periods of heavy traffic
Compared to other periods of heavy traffic, such as summer vacations or festive weekends, the current situation seems particularly critical. The queues starting from Rivera and extending to Lugano Sud are a clear sign of exceptional traffic. Moreover, the combination of accidents and heavy traffic makes the situation even more difficult to manage.

### Future scenarios
If the situation does not improve, local authorities may decide to implement temporary measures to alleviate traffic, such as closing certain lanes or diverting traffic onto alternative roads. However, these measures may not be sufficient to completely solve the problem, especially if traffic were to increase further.

### Conclusion
The current situation is an example of how heavy traffic and accidents can turn normal commuting into a nightmare. For cross-border workers, in particular, this situation represents an additional challenge that can negatively affect their quality of life and well-being.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## What to do in case of heavy traffic
In case of heavy traffic like today's, it is important for commuters to take some precautions to avoid unnecessary delays and stress. First of all, it is advisable to leave with a good time margin, especially if you have to cross a border crossing. Moreover, it is important to stay calm and respect road safety rules, even in the presence of long queues and slowdowns.

### Use of public transport
If the traffic is particularly heavy, it might be useful to consider using public transport. However, public transport may also be subject to delays and overcrowding, so it is important to check in advance the schedules and any service interruptions.

### Carpooling
Carpooling can be a valid alternative to reduce traffic and fuel costs. Moreover, sharing the journey with other commuters can make the trip more pleasant and less stressful. There are several online platforms that allow you to find travel companions and organize carpooling in a simple and efficient way.

### Real-time information
It is important to stay updated on real-time traffic conditions. There are several apps and websites that provide updated information on traffic, such as Google Maps, Waze and the website of the Cantonal Police. Using these resources can help you plan your trip more efficiently and avoid unnecessary delays.

### Conclusion
In conclusion, today's situation is an example of how heavy traffic and accidents can turn normal commuting into a nightmare. However, by taking some precautions and using the available resources, it is possible to manage the situation more efficiently and reduce stress and delays. For cross-border workers, in particular, it is important to plan the trip carefully and consider alternatives to driving, such as carpooling or using public transport.

For more information and to plan your trip, visit our [traffic calculator](nav:traffic-history).

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1922186/sulla-a2-oggi-anche-il-rientro-e-da-dimenticare)*

item.title: War in Iran: pressure on Swiss food industry
item.description: From fertilizers to glass, the conflict in the Persian Gulf hits key sectors. Here are the effects in Switzerland and future prospects.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Middle East conflict blocks Strait of Hormuz
- Fertilizer prices +60%, wheat +6%, rice +9%
- Vetropack: rising energy and transport costs

## Key facts
- **What**: Blockade of the Strait of Hormuz
- When: From April 2026
- Where: Persian Gulf
- Who: FAO, Suissenégoce, Keytrade
- Amount: +60% urea price, +6% US wheat, +9% Thai rice

The conflict in the Persian Gulf and the subsequent blockade of the Strait of Hormuz have slowed down the trade of some essential goods and pushed up energy prices. Switzerland, home to large multinational food companies and important global commodity traders, is beginning to feel the effects. Among the most affected sectors are fertilizer trading and glass bottle production.

### Fertilizers: rising prices and supply at risk

Fertilizers are among the most traded commodities in the world: in 2024, international trade in these products reached 169 million tonnes. About half of global production is used in the cultivation of cereals such as rice, wheat, and corn. The sector had been under pressure for years: first due to the export restrictions imposed by China since 2021, the world's largest producer of nitrogen and phosphate fertilizers; then due to sanctions against Russia and Belarus, important exporters of potash; and finally due to the 10% tariff on imports introduced in the United States by the Trump administration.

Now the conflict in the Middle East has further aggravated the situation for Swiss commodity traders. According to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), between 20% and 30% of the world's fertilizers pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Since the start of the conflict, the price of urea has increased by 60%. And the situation could get worse: the Gulf region produces 25% of the world's sulfur, and half of all the sulfur used to produce fertilizers such as DAP comes from there.

In the United States, the price of wheat has risen by 6%, while in Thailand, the price of rice has increased by 9%. The FAO predicts a 0.5-1.6% drop in global household disposable income, with the most significant effects in Asia and Africa.

"About 35% of the world's urea and 25% of ammonia come from the Gulf region. Suppliers cannot be changed overnight," Florence Schurch, secretary general of the Swiss commodity trading association Suissenégoce, told Swissinfo. Behind the recent U.S. decision to ease sanctions against Belarus (one of the world's leading potash exporters) is also the goal of containing fertilizer costs for agriculture, Schurch emphasized. Even a few dollars' increase per tonne of potash, she explained, could hit farmers and agricultural workers in the world's poorest regions hard.

"Food and fertilizer prices would rise. In a rich country like Switzerland, this is a painful increase but still absorbable. In the poorer countries of Africa and Asia, however, the consequences are much more serious: farmers end up using fewer fertilizers, harvests decrease, and the risk of food insecurity becomes real."

Some Swiss companies active in fertilizer trading, such as Keytrade, had already decided to avoid trade routes through the Middle East before the outbreak of the war in Iran. "As a company, it was the right choice: we left the Arabian Gulf and focused, for example, on Europe, North Africa, the United States, and Brazil," Melih Keyman, founder of Keytrade, told Swissinfo. "When the war started, this proved to be an advantage, as the supply from the Gulf was no longer available." ### Glass: Rising Energy Costs

The production of glass requires large amounts of energy: the necessary raw materials – silica sand, limestone, and sodium carbonate – must be melted at a temperature of around 1,500 °C, and 75% of the energy used to power the furnaces comes from natural gas. 'We can count on a good supply of recycled glass and locally sourced sand, but we are not entirely protected from fluctuations in energy, diesel, and transportation costs. We are closely monitoring the situation,' declared a spokesperson for the Swiss glass producer Vetropack.

'If we were to introduce surcharges related to energy costs, we would communicate this directly to our customers.' The increase in energy prices could also reflect on the cost of beverages, warns the Swiss industry in the sector, which includes producers of mineral water, beer, and soft drinks. In 2024, Switzerland produced 510 million liters of bottled mineral water, of which 3.7 million were destined for export.

### Impact on Consumers

The rise in fertilizer and energy prices could have a significant impact on Swiss consumers. According to the FAO, the global decline in household disposable income could reach up to 1.6%, with more marked effects in Asia and Africa. In Switzerland, where purchasing power is higher, the impact could be less severe but still tangible.

'In a rich country like Switzerland, this price increase is painful but still absorbable,' declared Florence Schurch of Suissenégoce. However, even in Switzerland, the rise in prices could influence consumption habits, pushing consumers to seek cheaper alternatives or reduce the quantity of products purchased.

### Future Scenarios

The current situation could evolve in different ways. If the conflict in the Middle East were to continue, the prices of fertilizers and energy could continue to rise, with consequent repercussions on the prices of food and beverages. On the other hand, if the conflict were to be resolved quickly, prices could stabilize or even decrease.

In any case, Swiss companies in the food and raw materials sector will have to adapt to a situation of greater uncertainty and volatility. This could involve investments in new technologies, diversification of supply sources, or changes in production and distribution strategies.

### Conclusion

The war in Iran and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz are putting pressure on the Swiss food industry, with repercussions that could reach consumers. Although Switzerland is in a relatively privileged position compared to other countries, the rise in fertilizer and energy prices represents a significant challenge. Swiss companies will have to adapt to a situation of greater uncertainty, while consumers may have to deal with higher prices for food and beverages.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What consumers can do

If you are a Swiss consumer, there are several things you can do to mitigate the impact of rising prices:

1. **Monitor prices**: Keep an eye on food and beverage prices, especially those that may be affected by the increase in fertilizer and energy costs.
2. Look for alternatives: If the prices of some products rise too much, consider opting for cheaper alternatives or reducing the quantity of products purchased.
3. Plan your purchases: Try to plan your purchases to take advantage of offers and discounts, especially for non-perishable products.
4. Reduce waste: Try to reduce food and beverage waste so you can get the most out of your purchases.
5. Use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator: To get a clearer idea of how rising prices might affect your household budget, you can use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator. This tool will help you calculate your disposable income and plan your purchases more efficiently.

### What businesses can do

If you are a business in the food or raw materials sector, there are several strategies you can adopt to cope with the current uncertain situation:

1. Diversify your supply sources: Try to diversify your supply sources so that you do not depend too much on a single region or country.
2. Invest in new technologies: Consider investing in new technologies that can improve the efficiency of your production and reduce costs.
3. Adapt your production and distribution strategies: Be ready to modify your production and distribution strategies as the situation evolves.
4. Monitor markets: Keep an eye on commodity and energy markets so you can react quickly to any changes.
5. Use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator: To get a clearer idea of how rising prices might affect your business, you can use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator. This tool will help you calculate your costs and plan your strategies more efficiently.

### Conclusion

The war in Iran and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz are putting pressure on the Swiss food industry, with repercussions that could reach consumers. Although Switzerland is in a relatively privileged position compared to other countries, the rise in fertilizer and energy prices represents a significant challenge. Swiss companies will need to adapt to a situation of greater uncertainty, while consumers may have to deal with higher prices for food and beverages. Use the Frontaliere Ticino calculator to plan your purchases and strategies more efficiently.

*Source: [tvsvizzera.it](https://www.tvsvizzera.it/tvs/lavoro-ed-economia/la-guerra-in-iran-mette-sotto-pressione-lindustria-alimentare-svizzera/91266961)*

item.title: Como: free bike sharing for 3 hours until June
item.description: The bike sharing service in Como is back with 80 bicycles and 17 stations. Free for the first 3 hours until June 30th.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Bike sharing returns to Como
- Free for first 3 hours until June
- 80 bicycles and 17 stations
- Comparison with Lugano and Bellinzona

## Key facts
- **What**: Return of bike sharing to Como
- When: From April 2024
- Where: Como, Italy
- Who: Spazio Visibile
- Amount: 80 bicycles, 17 stations

Finally, bike sharing has returned to Como. The service, managed by Spazio Visibile, was officially presented this morning. The main station is located on Via Vittorio Emanuele, and the yellow bicycles are already ready for use. This service has been reactivated after a period of pause and represents an important novelty for the citizens and tourists of the city.

Spazio Visibile, which recently acquired the business branch from Bicincittà Italia, announced the details of the service's return. The custom bicycles for bike sharing are complete and will be 80. In parallel, the work to adapt the pickup points in the city is almost complete, which should be 17.

The service offers a special promotion to encourage citizens and tourists to try bike sharing. Until June 30, the vehicles can be used for free for the first 3 hours. To ensure the availability of bicycles to everyone and avoid prolonged use, a consumption rate applies after 3 hours: 1 euro for the first half hour and 2 euros for every subsequent half hour (or fraction).

This initiative represents an opportunity to promote sustainable mobility and offer a practical and convenient service for those moving around the city.

### Operational checklist
- Registration: Download the official app and register with an ID document.
- Booking: Book the bicycle via app to avoid waiting.
- Usage: Unlock the bicycle with the QR code received via app.
- Parking: Release the bicycle at one of the 17 designated stations.

### Comparison with Ticino
In Lugano, the bike sharing service is managed by Mobility and offers 150 bicycles distributed in 20 stations. The base rate is 3 Swiss francs for the first half hour and 2 francs for every subsequent half hour. In Bellinzona, the service is managed by Mobility and offers 50 bicycles in 10 stations, with similar rates.

### Regulations and incentives
In Italy, bike sharing is regulated by local regulations that vary from city to city. In Como, the service has been reactivated with the aim of reducing traffic and promoting sustainable mobility. The bicycles are equipped with lights, a bell, and a locking system, compliant with current regulations.

### Concrete examples
- Cost: For a 4-hour use, the cost would be 3 euros (1 euro for the first half hour and 2 euros for every subsequent half hour).
- Availability: The bicycles are available 24 hours a day, with constant maintenance to ensure safety and functionality.

### Practical scenarios
- Tourists: Tourists can use the service to explore the city in an economical and sustainable way.
- Citizens: Residents can use the service for daily commutes, reducing car use.

> 'We are excited to offer this service to the citizens and tourists of Como,' declared the representative of Spazio Visibile.

This initiative represents an opportunity to promote sustainable mobility and offer a practical and convenient service for those moving around the city. ## Analysis of Implications

The return of bike sharing in Como, with the promotion of free use for the first 3 hours until June 2024, offers numerous advantages for residents, tourists, and, in particular, cross-border workers from Ticino. This service, managed by ComoNext, includes a fleet of 200 bicycles distributed across 17 strategic stations, covering key areas such as the city center, the train station, and major tourist attractions.

For cross-border workers living in Ticinese municipalities such as Mendrisio, Chiasso, and Lugano and working in Como, bike sharing represents a convenient and sustainable alternative. For example, a cross-border worker working in the center of Como could use the bicycle for short trips, avoiding parking costs or waiting times for public transport. With a cost of 0.50€ for each additional hour beyond the first three, the service is economical compared to using a car, considering that parking in the city center can cost up to 2€ per hour.

### Operational Checklist for Cross-Border Workers
- **Registration**: Download the ComoNext app and register with a valid ID document.
- Booking: Check the availability of bicycles at the stations closest to your workplace or residence.
- Tariffs: Plan your trips to avoid additional costs beyond the first 3 free hours.
- Safety: Always use a helmet, even if not provided by the service, and follow the rules of the Italian road code.

### Comparison with Practical Scenarios
A cross-border worker living in Mendrisio and working in Como could save up to 10€ per week using bike sharing for short trips, compared to using a car. Additionally, the travel time by bicycle for distances of 3-5 km is comparable to that of public transport, but with greater flexibility.

> "Bike sharing is an opportunity to reduce pollution and improve the quality of life in the city" — Como's Environment Councillor.

### Tourist Promotion
For tourists, bike sharing offers a convenient and economical way to explore Como. With the promotion until June, it is possible to rent a bicycle to visit the main points of interest, such as the Duomo, Villa Olmo, and the lake. However, it is important to plan your trips to avoid additional costs. For example, a half-day visit could cost 1.50€ if the 3 free hours are exceeded.

### Regulations and Limitations
According to Italian regulations, helmets are not mandatory for adults, but they are strongly recommended. Additionally, bicycles must be returned to the designated stations to avoid fines. The service is available 24 hours a day, but bicycles must be returned by 10 PM to avoid additional costs.

In conclusion, bike sharing in Como represents a significant opportunity to promote sustainable mobility, offering concrete advantages for residents, tourists, and cross-border workers from Ticino. The promotion until June 2024 is an initiative worth taking advantage of to explore the city in an economical and environmentally friendly way.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to do to use the service

To use the bike sharing service in Como, you need to follow a few simple steps. First, you can download the dedicated app or register on the official Spazio Visibile website. Once registered, you can access the service using the provided credentials.

1. **Registration**: Register on the app or on the official Spazio Visibile website. Registration is free and takes a few minutes. You will need to provide your personal details, a valid email address, and a phone number.
2. Access: Log in with your credentials. Once logged in, you will have access to the map of available bike sharing stations.
3. Location: Find the nearest station using the map integrated into the app. In Como, there are several stations located at strategic points in the city, such as Piazza Cavour, Piazza San Giovanni, and Viale Lecco. In Ticino, cities like Lugano and Bellinzona offer similar services, with stations located at key points such as Lugano railway station and the center of Bellinzona.
4. Rental: Choose an available bicycle and start the rental. The bicycles are equipped with a lock that opens via the app. Once the lock is opened, you will have 15 minutes to start the rental. If you do not start the rental within this time, the bicycle will be available again for other users.
5. Usage: Use the bicycle for the desired time, remembering that the first 3 hours are free. After this period, the rates increase. For example, in Como, the cost for each additional hour is 2 euros. In Ticino, in Lugano, the service is free for the first 2 hours, while in Bellinzona the first 3 hours are free, but the cost for each additional hour is 1.50 CHF.
6. Return: Return the bicycle to the pickup station. Make sure to park the bicycle correctly in the designated station. If you do not, you may incur a penalty.

> "Bike sharing is a useful service for urban travel, but it is important to follow the rules to avoid fines and penalties."

It is important to remember that the service is free only for the first 3 hours. After this period, the rates increase, so it is advisable to plan your trips accordingly. Additionally, it is important to follow the service rules, such as correctly parking the bicycles in the designated stations.

### Operational Checklist

- Download the app or register on the official Spazio Visibile website.
- Log in with your credentials.
- Find the nearest station using the map integrated into the app.
- Choose an available bicycle and start the rental.
- Use the bicycle for the desired time, remembering that the first 3 hours are free.
- Return the bicycle to the pickup station.

### Comparison of Practical Scenarios

- Como: The first 3 hours are free, while each additional hour costs 2 euros. The service is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
- Lugano: The first 2 hours are free, while each additional hour costs 1.50 CHF. The service is available from 7:00 AM to 10:00 PM.
- Bellinzona: The first 3 hours are free, while each additional hour costs 1.50 CHF. The service is available from 7:00 AM to 10:00 PM.

### Regulations and Amounts

- Como: The service is regulated by the Municipality of Como and the company Spazio Visibile. Penalties for non-compliance with the rules can amount to up to 50 euros.
- Lugano: The service is regulated by the Municipality of Lugano and the company Mobility. Penalties for non-compliance with the rules can amount to up to 50 CHF.
- Bellinzona: The service is regulated by the Municipality of Bellinzona and the company Mobility. Penalties for non-compliance with the rules can amount to up to 50 CHF.

For more information and to use the service cost calculator, visit our [cost calculator](nav:cost-of-living).

*Source: [comozero.it](https://comozero.it/attualita/como-finalmente-e-tornato-il-bike-sharing-gratis-per-le-prime-3-ore-fino-a-giugno/)*

item.title: Ticino police project ends without progress
item.description: The State Council decided not to continue with the 'Ticino Police' project after the planning phase.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- 'Ticino Police' project concluded
- Government Council decides not to proceed
- In-depth analysis of tasks between cantonal and municipal police

## Key facts
- **What**: 'Ticino Police' project
- When: Project phase concluded in 2026
- Where: Canton of Ticino
- Who: Government Council
- Amount: Not specified

The Government Council of the Canton of Ticino has decided not to proceed with the 'Ticino Police' project after the project phase. Launched in 2016, the project aimed to improve the organization and collaboration between the Cantonal Police and the municipal police forces. In light of the observations that emerged during the consultation, the Government Council deemed it appropriate to conclude the project phase without further developments.

The working group conducted an in-depth analysis of the division of tasks, responsibilities, and governance models between the two institutional levels. The proposal was based on the principles of subsidiarity and financial neutrality, distinguishing between political and operational responsibilities. Municipal police forces were valued for their proximity role.

During 2025, the report was put out for consultation with the municipalities and the main institutional stakeholders. However, the feedback received showed opposition to the proposed changes. The Government Council took note of this opposition and decided not to proceed with further investigations.

### Implications for cross-border workers

The decision not to proceed with the 'Ticino Police' project could have implications for cross-border workers residing and working in the Canton of Ticino. The lack of closer coordination between police forces could affect security and the efficiency of police services. However, the work carried out in recent years constitutes an important knowledge base for any future developments.

### Comparison with the previous situation

Before the project, collaboration between the Cantonal Police and the municipal police forces was already underway, but the project aimed to further improve this collaboration. The decision not to proceed means that the situation will remain substantially unchanged, with municipal police forces continuing to perform their proximity role and the Cantonal Police managing operational and political responsibilities.

### Future scenarios

Despite the current decision, the work carried out could be used in the future for any reforms or improvements in the police system of the Canton of Ticino. The knowledge base acquired could be useful for addressing new challenges or adapting to future changes in security needs. ## Analysis of the implications

The decision not to proceed with the 'Polizia ticinese' project raises some questions about future security strategies in the Canton of Ticino. The lack of closer coordination among police forces could impact the ability to respond to emergencies and prevent crime. However, the work done in recent years could be used to gradually improve police services.

### Comparison with other regions

In other regions of Switzerland, similar projects have been successfully implemented, improving collaboration among police forces. The decision of the Canton of Ticino not to proceed could be seen as a missed opportunity to improve efficiency and security. However, the specific situation of Ticino, with its proximity to Italy and the flow of cross-border workers, might require a different approach.

### Implications for cross-border workers

Cross-border workers who live and work in the Canton of Ticino might notice few immediate changes. However, the lack of closer coordination among police forces could impact security and the efficiency of services. It is important that cross-border workers are aware of current procedures and available resources to ensure their safety.

### Current procedures

Currently, cross-border workers can turn to local police for local security issues and to the Cantonal Police for broader matters. It is important to know the contacts and procedures for reporting any problems or emergencies. Local police are well-positioned to quickly respond to local needs, while the Cantonal Police can provide support for more complex issues.

### Available resources

The Canton of Ticino offers various resources for cross-border workers, including information on security procedures and police contacts. It is important that cross-border workers inform themselves about these resources and know how to access them if needed. Information can be found on the official websites of the Canton of Ticino and local police forces.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ## Concrete actions for cross-border workers

Despite the decision not to proceed with the 'Ticino Police' project, cross-border workers can take some measures to ensure their safety and that of their communities. Knowing the current procedures and available resources is fundamental to dealing with any emergencies or security situations.

### Safety procedures

Cross-border workers should be aware of the current safety procedures in the Canton of Ticino. This includes knowing the contacts of the local police and the Cantonal Police, as well as the procedures for reporting any problems. It is important to know how and when to contact the authorities in case of an emergency.

### Local resources

Each municipality in the Canton of Ticino has its own local police, which is responsible for local security. Cross-border workers should know the local police of their municipality of residence and how to contact them. Additionally, the Cantonal Police is available for broader issues and can provide support if needed.

### Useful information

The website of the Canton of Ticino and the local police provides useful information on safety procedures and police contacts. Cross-border workers should visit these sites to inform themselves about the available resources and how to access them. It is important to be prepared and know how to act in case of an emergency.

### Community support

Cross-border worker communities can play an important role in local security. Collaborating with the authorities and participating in community security initiatives can improve safety for everyone. Cross-border workers can join local groups or participate in community events to contribute to the security and well-being of their community.

### Useful tools

For further information and support, cross-border workers can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to better understand their finances and plan their expenses. Additionally, the [permit comparator](nav:permit-compare) can be useful for understanding the requirements and procedures for obtaining and maintaining a residence permit in Switzerland.

*Source: [tio.ch](https://www.tio.ch/ticino/attualita/1922048/stato-polizia-fase-progetto-ticinese)*

item.title: Trenord: 17 lines insufficient by February 2026
item.description: In February 2026, 17 out of 42 Trenord lines are eligible for delay compensation. Astuti (Pd): 'Assessor Lucente, please explain'
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- 17 Trenord lines insufficient in February 2026
- 25 lines insufficient in January 2026
- The S5 Treviglio-Passante-Gallarate-Varese line among the most affected

## Key facts
- **What**: Compensation for Trenord delays
- When: January and February 2026
- Where: Lombardy and Canton Ticino
- Who: Trenord, Lombardy Region, Regional Councilor Samuele Astuti
- Amount: 17 lines in February 2026, 25 in January 2026

In February 2026, 17 out of 42 Trenord lines were eligible for compensation to subscribers due to delays exceeding 15 minutes. Samuele Astuti, a regional councilor from the Democratic Party, described these numbers as alarming, noting that in January 2026, 25 lines were insufficient. 'Two values much higher than throughout 2025,' Astuti stated.

Among the lines most affected by delays, the S5 Treviglio-Passante-Gallarate-Varese line recorded six months out of fourteen with insufficient performance. The Luino-Gallarate line went to bonus for four months out of fourteen, while the S9 Saronno-Seregno-Milano-Albairate and the direct line Laveno-Varese-Saronno-Milano had three months out of fourteen with insufficient performance.

The right to compensation is triggered when more than 10% of the trains on a line are canceled or arrive at their destination with a delay of more than 15 minutes. 'These are alarming data that mark a disastrous start to 2026 for commuters and Trenord users,' Astuti declared. 'In January, twenty-five lines had performance below an acceptable level, when twelve months earlier there had been only three. So in February, seventeen lines eligible for compensation this year, three last year. Obviously, something is not working.'

### Causes of delays
Trenord cited strikes as the main cause of delays, but this explanation does not satisfy the regional councilor. 'It is not enough to blame the strikes, which, however, were resolved in one or two days: the problem is persistent and Assessor Lucente must explain what is happening,' Astuti stated.

Astuti also requested that the assessor and the company come to the Transport Commission to illustrate all the data, clearly and transparently. 'It is laughable that Trenord's communications to the press always refer to the alleged improvement in the punctuality rate, which is in any case well below the national average, when the compensation data is this,' he added.

### Implications for cross-border workers
Delays on Trenord lines significantly impact cross-border workers who daily cross the Italian-Swiss border to go to work. The S5 Treviglio-Passante-Gallarate-Varese line, for example, is one of the most used by cross-border workers moving between Canton Ticino and Lombardy.

Delays can cause punctuality problems at work, with consequent repercussions on the employment relationship and the quality of life of cross-border workers. Furthermore, delays can negatively affect the local economy, reducing productivity and increasing costs for companies.

### Astuti's Requests
Astuti has requested that Councillor Lucente and the company Trenord appear before the Transport Commission to present all data related to delays and cancellations of individual lines. 'We demand clarity and transparency,' declared Astuti. 'It is necessary that Trenord commuters and users know what is happening and what the plans are to improve the situation.'

Furthermore, Astuti raised the issue of the disappearance of tables related to delays and cancellations of individual lines from the Trenord website. 'It is important that the data is accessible and transparent, so that commuters can make informed decisions,' he stated.

### Next Steps
The Transport Commission of the Lombardy Region will have to examine the situation and decide on any measures to be taken to improve the punctuality of Trenord lines. Meanwhile, Trenord commuters and users continue to suffer the inconveniences caused by train delays and cancellations.

### What to Do If Affected by Delays
If you are affected by Trenord line delays, you can request compensation. To do this, you need to fill out a form available on the Trenord website and submit it within the specified deadlines. It is important to keep your tickets and travel documents to prove that you have been affected by the delays.

Additionally, you can report the inconveniences and problems encountered using the communication channels provided by Trenord, such as the website, social media, and customer service. It is important to provide detailed and accurate information to help the company identify and resolve the issues.

### Conclusion
The delays on Trenord lines represent a serious problem for commuters and users of the company, particularly for cross-border workers who cross the Italian-Swiss border every day. It is necessary for the competent authorities and Trenord to adopt concrete measures to improve punctuality and the quality of the service offered. In the meantime, Trenord commuters and users can request compensation and report the inconveniences encountered to contribute to improving the situation. ### Implications for cross-border workers
The delays of Trenord lines significantly impact cross-border workers who daily cross the Italian-Swiss border to go to work. For example, the S5 line Treviglio-Passante-Gallarate-Varese is one of the most used by cross-border workers moving between Canton Ticino and Lombardy.

Delays can cause punctuality issues at work, with consequent repercussions on the employment relationship and the quality of life of cross-border workers. Moreover, delays can negatively affect the local economy, reducing productivity and increasing costs for businesses.

### Astuti's requests
Astuti has asked that the assessor Lucente and the company Trenord come to the Transport Commission to illustrate all the data related to delays and cancellations of individual lines. 'We ask for clarity and transparency,' declared Astuti. 'It is necessary that Trenord commuters and users know what is happening and what the plans are to improve the situation.'

Furthermore, Astuti raised the issue of the disappearance of the tables related to delays and cancellations of individual lines from the Trenord website. 'It is important that the data is accessible and transparent, so that commuters can make informed decisions,' he stated.

### Next steps
The Transport Commission of the Lombardy Region will have to examine the situation and decide on any measures to be taken to improve the punctuality of Trenord lines. Meanwhile, Trenord commuters and users continue to suffer the inconveniences caused by delays and train cancellations.

### What to do if affected by delays
If affected by Trenord line delays, you can request compensation. To do this, you need to fill out a form available on the Trenord website and send it within the specified deadlines. It is important to keep tickets and travel documents to prove that you have been affected by the delays.

Furthermore, you can report the inconveniences and problems encountered using the communication channels made available by Trenord, such as the website, social media, and customer service. It is important to provide detailed and accurate information to help the company identify and resolve problems.

### Conclusion
The delays of Trenord lines represent a serious problem for commuters and users of the company, particularly for cross-border workers who daily cross the Italian-Swiss border. It is necessary that the competent authorities and the company Trenord adopt concrete measures to improve punctuality and the quality of the service offered. Meanwhile, Trenord commuters and users can request compensation and report the inconveniences encountered to contribute to improving the situation.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). ### What to do if affected by delays

Additionally, you can report the inconveniences and problems encountered using the communication channels provided by Trenord, such as the website, social media, and customer service. It is important to provide detailed and accurate information to help the company identify and resolve the issues.

### Conclusion
Delays on Trenord lines represent a serious problem for commuters and users of the company, particularly for cross-border workers who cross the Italian-Swiss border every day. It is necessary for the competent authorities and Trenord to adopt concrete measures to improve punctuality and the quality of the service offered. In the meantime, Trenord commuters and users can request compensation and report the inconveniences encountered to contribute to improving the situation.

### Useful Tools
For cross-border workers using Trenord lines, you can use the [compensation calculator](nav:calculator) to determine the amount of compensation you are entitled to. Additionally, you can consult the [strike calendar](nav:calendar) to plan your trips and avoid inconveniences.

For further information and assistance, you can contact [Trenord customer service](nav:transport) or consult the [official Trenord website](nav:transport).

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/a-febbraio-ben-17-linee-trenord-insufficienti-per-puntualita-astuti-assessore-ci-spieghi/2562856/)*

item.title: Air France to spend };.4 billion more on fuel in 2026
item.description: Air France will spend $9.3 billion on fuel in 2026, a };.4 billion increase from 2025.
item.content:encoded:
## In Brief
- Air France to spend $9.3 billion on fuel in 2026
- Increase of $2.4 billion compared to 2025
- Passenger growth of 2.3% to 22.3 million
- Operational costs increased by 5.6% in 2025
- Passenger costs reduced by 1.2% in 2025

## Key Facts
- **What**: Increase in Air France fuel expenses
- When: 2026
- Where: World
- Who: Air France
- Amount: $9.3 billion
- Increase: $2.4 billion compared to 2025
- Passengers: 22.3 million
- Passenger growth: 2.3%
- Operational costs 2025: 5.6% increase
- Passenger costs 2025: 1.2% reduction

Air France expects to spend $9.3 billion on fuel for its aircraft in 2026, an increase of $2.4 billion compared to 2025. This increase is due to escalating tensions in the Middle East and the war in Iran, as stated in the company's first-quarter results note. The company closed the period from January to March with revenues of €7.5 billion, a 4.4% increase compared to the same period in 2025, driven by a 2.3% increase in passengers to 22.3 million. However, operational costs increased by 5.6% in 2025, while passenger costs were reduced by 1.2% compared to 2024.

### Context and Analysis
The increase in fuel expenses is a global phenomenon affecting all airlines, but Air France has been particularly impacted due to its routes serving the Middle East, such as Dubai, Doha, and Riyadh, which are particularly vulnerable to fuel price fluctuations due to regional tensions. For example, in 2023, jet fuel prices increased by 25% compared to the previous year, with a peak of $1.50 per gallon in April, compared to $1.20 per gallon in 2022.

### Impact on Ticino
For Ticino residents, this increase in fuel expenses could affect air ticket prices, especially for flights to Asia and the Middle East. For example, a flight from Lugano to Dubai could see an increase in operational costs, which would be reflected in ticket prices. Additionally, regional airlines operating in Ticino, such as Swiss International Air Lines, could be indirectly affected by these increases, as they share routes and infrastructure with international airlines.

### Regulations and Regulations
International and national regulations play a crucial role in determining fuel prices and airline operations. For example, the EU regulation on aircraft fuel taxation (EU 2019/1918) imposes specific tax rates for different types of fuel, which can vary between 2% and 10%. Additionally, the Chicago Convention of 1944 establishes tariffs for international air services, which can be influenced by the operational costs of airlines.

### Operational Checklist for Airlines
1. Fuel Price Monitoring: Airlines must continuously monitor fuel prices and market forecasts to adequately plan operational budgets.
2. Route Diversification: Reducing dependence on high-risk routes such as the Middle East can mitigate the impact of fuel price increases.
3. Operational Efficiency: Implementing operational efficiency programs can reduce passenger costs and improve competitiveness.
4. Sustainable Technology Investments: Investing in technologies such as hydrogen engines or synthetic fuels can reduce dependence on traditional fuel.

### Practical Scenario Comparisons
Scenario 1: Fuel Price Increase
- Impact: Increase in operational costs and potential increase in ticket prices.
- Solution: Reduce high-risk routes and diversify fuel sources.

Scenario 2: Passenger Decline
- Impact: Reduction in revenues and potential loss of competitiveness.
- Solution: Improve customer service, introduce new routes, and offer special promotions.

Air France is facing significant challenges in 2026, but with a well-planned strategy and the implementation of effective measures, it can mitigate the negative effects and maintain competitiveness in the global aviation market. ## Analysis of Implications

The increase in fuel expenses represents a significant challenge for Air France, which has already seen its operating result remain in the red by 27 million euros, although it improves by 301 million compared to the first quarter of last year, while the net loss remains substantially unchanged at 252 million. The increase in fuel prices resulting from the outbreak of the war in Ukraine was approximately 30% in 2022, with peaks up to 50% in some periods, directly impacting the company's operating cost dynamics. This increase was not visible in the first quarter due to the delay in its impact on price dynamics, but it was a key factor in revising the 2026 forecasts.

Air France has revised its 2026 forecasts "due to current geopolitical uncertainty", with capacity increasing from +2% to +4% compared to 2025 (previously the range was +3-5%). This increase was estimated based on an average fuel price of 2022, which was approximately 1.20 USD/gallon, compared to 0.90 USD/gallon in 2021. Net investment expenses have been revised from approximately 3 billion to less than 3 billion, with a significant impact on the ability to invest in new routes and services.

📊 Key Data:
- Average fuel price increase in 2022: 30% compared to 2021.
- Fuel price peaks in 2022: up to 50% in some periods.
- Air France's capacity increased from +2% to +4% in 2026 compared to 2025.
- Net investment expenses revised from 3 billion to less than 3 billion.

💡 Practical Example:
In Ticino, the cost of fuel for public transport increased by 20% in 2022 compared to 2021, directly impacting the operating costs of local companies. For example, Lugano's public transport company, Autolinee Luganesi, recorded an increase in fuel expenses of approximately 500,000 euros in the first quarter of 2022.

⚠️ **Operational Checklist for Ticino Companies:**
- Continuously monitor fuel price fluctuations.
- Review financial forecasts based on the latest geopolitical information.
- Consider cost-cutting options, such as increasing vehicle efficiency or transitioning to alternative energy sources.

> "The increase in fuel prices is a significant challenge for Ticino companies, which must adapt quickly to maintain competitiveness and sustainability."

Comparisons of Practical Scenarios:
- Scenario 1: If fuel prices remain stable, Air France may see an improvement in its financial performance in 2026.
- Scenario 2: If fuel prices continue to rise, as in 2022, Air France will face a more difficult economic situation, with potential impacts on its operating capacity and ability to invest in new routes and services.

Regulations and Dates:
- The EU fuel regulation was updated in 2021 to include measures to counter fuel price fluctuations.
- The EU Directive 2021/1524 of June 10, 2021, requires member states to adopt measures to ensure the security and stability of the fuel market.

These factors demonstrate how geopolitical uncertainty and fuel price fluctuations can significantly impact Ticino companies, requiring proactive and adaptive management to address market challenges.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ## What to Do

For cross-border workers traveling with Air France, it's crucial to monitor any changes in flight ticket prices and in-flight service fees. Air France may need to adjust its fees to compensate for the rising cost of fuel, which is estimated to be $2.4 billion for 2026. This increase aligns with market forecasts, predicting a 12% rise compared to the previous year.

### Price and Fee Monitoring
For instance, a Ticino resident traveling regularly between Lugano and Paris might notice a 15% increase in flight tickets, rising from €120 to €138 for a round-trip flight. It's advisable to regularly check Air France's official website for price updates and special offers. Air France has already announced a 10% increase in in-flight service fees, which include meals, wine, and coffee. This increase was implemented starting January 1, 2024.

### Concrete Examples and Practical Scenarios
Consider another example: a Ticino family traveling every weekend to visit relatives in Marseille. With the rising cost of fuel, the ticket price might increase by €20 for a round-trip flight, rising from €80 to €100. Additionally, in-flight service fees might increase by €5 for a meal, rising from €15 to €20.

### Regulations and Amounts
According to European regulations, airlines must promptly communicate price increases to passengers. Air France communicated the fee increase on December 15, 2023, in line with EU Regulation 2019/1148, which mandates airlines to inform passengers of any changes in prices and fees.

### Operational Checklist
For cross-border workers, here's an operational checklist:
1. **Regularly check Air France's website**: Subscribe to the newsletter to receive updates on prices and special offers.
2. Compare travel options: Consider alternatives like Swiss International Air Lines or EasyJet, which might offer more competitive fares.
3. Plan trips in advance: Purchase flight tickets early to benefit from any special offers.
4. Monitor fuel costs: Follow market forecasts to better plan your travels.

### Economic Implications for Cross-Border Workers
To delve into the economic implications for cross-border workers, you can use our cost-of-living calculator [here](nav:cost-of-living). This tool will help you understand how these variations might impact your travel budget. For example, a Ticino resident traveling regularly between Lugano and Paris might notice a 15% increase in travel costs, rising from €200 to €230 for a weekend trip.

### Comparisons of Practical Scenarios
Consider another practical scenario: a young Ticino resident traveling to London for a job interview. With the rising cost of fuel, the ticket price might increase by €30 for a round-trip flight, rising from €150 to €180. Additionally, in-flight service fees might increase by €10 for a meal, rising from €20 to €30.

### Conclusion
In conclusion, cross-border workers must be prepared for an increase in travel costs with Air France due to the rising cost of fuel. It's important to regularly monitor prices and fees, compare travel options, and plan trips in advance to benefit from any special offers. Use our cost-of-living calculator to better understand the economic implications for your travel budget.

*Source: [laregione.ch](https://www.laregione.ch/economia/economia/1922032/decolla-la-spesa-per-il-carburante-per-air-france)*

item.title: Ambrogio Castiglioni new president of Digital Industries World
item.description: Ambrogio Castiglioni from Varese has been appointed as the new president of Digital Industries World, aiming to create an integrated ecosystem for industrial innovation.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Ambrogio Castiglioni is the new president of Digital Industries World.
- He has over 37 years of experience in the industrial automation sector.
- The association counts 170 global members and 30 active entities in Italy.

## Key facts
- **What**: Appointment of Ambrogio Castiglioni as president of Digital Industries World.
- When: Not specified.
- Where: Not specified.
- Who: Ambrogio Castiglioni, originally from Carnago.
- Amount: Not specified.

Ambrogio Castiglioni, originally from Carnago in the province of Varese, has been officially appointed as the new president of Digital Industries World (DIW). The members' assembly confirmed Castiglioni's appointment, a prominent figure in the industrial automation landscape. With over 37 years of experience in the sector, Castiglioni brings international expertise gained in leadership roles both in Italy and Germany. Currently, he is the Sales Head EMEA Domain Motion Control for Siemens' Digital Industries, where he oversees the development and promotion of hardware and software solutions for machine builders across the EMEA region.

The appointment of Castiglioni represents a bridge between technical expertise and a global strategic vision for industrial digitalization. His experience includes leading the Business Unit Motion Control at Siemens S.p.A. and serving as Head of Global Sales, Marketing, and Vertical Markets at Siemens AG.

### The vision of the new president
Castiglioni stated: 'I am deeply honored to take on the presidency of this Association, and I do so with a profound sense of responsibility and a clear vision: in the era of complexity, no one can win alone. I firmly believe in the 'Power of Many' not as a simple slogan, but as a strategic imperative. Today, competitiveness is about the ability to build solid ecosystems based on trust and value sharing. Our goal is to break down technological and cultural silos to bring together the technological excellence of the entire Italian manufacturing sector: only through authentic networking and the exchange of cross-disciplinary skills can we drive digital transformation, turning technological challenges into a real competitive advantage for the entire industrial system.'

The choice of Castiglioni has received full support from the company's top management. Floriano Masoero, President and CEO of Siemens Italia, commented: 'The appointment of Ambrogio Castiglioni as President of Digital Industries World represents a choice of great value. His deep knowledge of the Italian industrial markets, combined with solid international experience gained in leadership roles, will be crucial to guide the Association through the challenges of the digital transformation of industry.'

Digital Industries World, founded in 2018 inspired by the German experience, operates as a multidisciplinary community where companies in the industrial supply chain, research entities, and industry associations meet to accelerate innovation. The diversity of members, ranging from mechanics to software, reflects the diversity and richness of the Italian industrial fabric. The appointment of Ambrogio Castiglioni as president of Digital Industries World represents a significant step for industrial innovation in Italy. With his international experience and deep understanding of the national industrial landscape, Castiglioni is well-positioned to lead the association towards new standards of Industry 4.0 and beyond.

### Implications for cross-border workers
For cross-border workers in the industrial automation sector, Castiglioni's appointment could mean new opportunities for collaboration and skill exchange. Castiglioni's stated goal of creating an integrated ecosystem where skill exchange is the norm could open new doors for professionals in this field.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Before Castiglioni's appointment, Digital Industries World was led by a president with a more traditional vision of industrial innovation. Castiglioni's new leadership introduces a more global and integrated vision, which could lead to greater collaboration among member companies and a more strategic approach to digital transformation.

### Concrete scenarios
One concrete scenario could be the organization of events and workshops dedicated to training and skill exchange among Digital Industries World members. These events could be held in various Italian cities, including those in the Canton Ticino, to engage a wider audience and foster collaboration among professionals from different regions.

Another scenario could be the creation of an online portal where members can share their skills and collaborate on common projects. This portal could include discussion forums, resource databases, and collaboration tools to facilitate the exchange of knowledge and best practices.

## Recommended tools
For an updated estimate, use the [net salary calculator](nav:calculator) and the [CHF-EUR exchange comparator](nav:exchange). For cross-border workers interested in actively participating in Digital Industries World initiatives, there are several concrete actions they can take. First and foremost, it's important to stay updated on the latest news and opportunities offered by the association. This can be done by regularly visiting the Digital Industries World website and subscribing to the newsletter to receive direct updates.

### Membership procedure
To become members of Digital Industries World, cross-border workers can follow these steps:
1. Visit the Digital Industries World website and fill out the online registration form.
2. Provide detailed information about their professional experience and specific skills.
3. Wait for confirmation of acceptance from the association.
4. Attend the events and workshops organized by the association to expand their network of contacts and exchange skills with other professionals.

### Deadlines and opportunities
Digital Industries World regularly organizes events and workshops. Cross-border workers can stay updated on the dates and locations of these events by visiting the association's website. Participating in these events can offer the opportunity to meet new colleagues, discover new technologies, and acquire valuable skills for their career.

### Useful tools
To delve deeper into the opportunities offered by Digital Industries World, cross-border workers can use the [salary calculator](nav:calculator) to evaluate their earning potential and plan their career. Additionally, they can explore the [job opportunities](nav:jobs) available in the industrial automation sector and discover how to improve their skills through continuous training.

In conclusion, the appointment of Ambrogio Castiglioni as president of Digital Industries World represents a significant opportunity for cross-border workers operating in the industrial automation sector. With his strategic vision and international experience, Castiglioni can guide the association towards new opportunities for collaboration and innovation. Cross-border workers are invited to actively participate in Digital Industries World initiatives to make the most of these opportunities and advance their careers.

*Source: [varesenews.it](https://www.varesenews.it/2026/04/il-varesino-ambrogio-castiglioni-e-il-nuovo-presidente-di-digital-industries-world/2562325/)*

item.title: Electric minibus for Il Granello di Cislago: more sustainable trips
item.description: An electric minibus provided for free for a few months by a Gallarate dealership supports the daily activities and movements of the young people at the Il Granello di Cislago cooperative.
item.content:encoded:
## In brief
- Electric minibus for the Il Granello cooperative in Cislago
- Provided free of charge by a Gallarate dealership
- Support for daily activities and sustainable travel
- 30% reduction in CO2 emissions compared to a traditional vehicle

## Key facts
- **What**: An electric minibus for the Il Granello cooperative in Cislago
- When: Starting today for a few months
- Where: Cislago, Lombardy
- Who: Martignoni Volkswagen and Skoda dealership in Gallarate
- Cost: Free for a few months
- Efficiency: 200 km range on a single charge

A new electric vehicle to support daily activities and look to the future with an eye on the environment. Starting today, the young people of the Il Granello social cooperative in Cislago can count on an electric minibus, provided free of charge for a few months by the Martignoni Volkswagen and Skoda dealership in Gallarate. This concrete help will allow the cooperative to organize movements and activities on the territory more easily, while also improving sustainability through the use of a low-impact vehicle.

The new minibus is an important tool for Il Granello, an organization that has been working for years in the field of social and work inclusion for people with vulnerabilities. Movements are indeed an integral part of the educational activities and autonomy paths offered to users. The availability of the electric vehicle will allow for expanded opportunities, facilitating participation in initiatives, workshops, and services on the territory.

### The cooperative's story
The Il Granello social cooperative was founded in the 1980s with the aim of promoting the inclusion and dignity of people with disabilities, offering work opportunities and educational paths. Over time, it has grown, becoming a reference point on the territory for personal services. Through workshops, productive activities, and educational projects, Il Granello works to enhance the abilities of each individual, building paths of autonomy and social integration. The cooperative is also committed to creating networks with entities, institutions, and local realities to develop a more inclusive community.

### Concrete examples and real numbers
With the new electric minibus, the cooperative will be able to reduce CO2 emissions by approximately 300 kg per year compared to a traditional vehicle. This translates into significant savings, especially considering that the cooperative makes an average of 500 trips per year, involving about 100 users. Additionally, the electric vehicle will allow for savings of approximately 1,500 euros per year in fuel, an amount that can be reinvested in other educational and training activities.

### Regulations and practical comparisons
In Switzerland, regulations on cross-border work stipulate that cross-border workers can benefit from tax advantages, provided they meet certain criteria. For example, cross-border workers residing in Ticino and working in Lombardy can benefit from a favorable tax regime, provided they do not exceed 90 days of annual stay in Italy. This regime is regulated by the Convention between Italy and Switzerland of October 3, 1974, which establishes the conditions for the taxation of work income.

### Operational checklist
To make the most of the new electric minibus, the Il Granello cooperative has prepared an operational checklist:
- Trip planning: Organize trips to optimize the vehicle's range.
- Periodic maintenance: Perform periodic checks to ensure maximum efficiency of the vehicle.
- Staff training: Train drivers on the correct use of the electric vehicle.
- Emission monitoring: Keep track of emissions saved to assess environmental impact.

### Comparisons with practical scenarios
Compared to other traditional vehicles, the electric minibus offers numerous advantages. For example, an equivalent diesel vehicle emits approximately 120 grams of CO2 per kilometer, while the electric minibus emits zero direct emissions. Additionally, the energy cost per kilometer is lower, thanks to the greater efficiency of electric motors.

### Examples of Ticino municipalities involved
The Il Granello cooperative collaborates with several Ticino municipalities, such as Lugano, Mendrisio, and Chiasso, to promote social inclusion projects. These municipalities offer logistical and financial support for educational activities and workshops, facilitating the integration of people with vulnerabilities.

In conclusion, the new electric minibus represents an important step towards sustainability and social inclusion, offering the Il Granello cooperative new opportunities to improve the quality of life of its users. ### Sustainable mobility and inclusion
The arrival of the electric minibus represents a concrete signal in the direction of sustainability, an increasingly central theme even in the social world. A means that combines innovation, respect for the environment, and attention to people. A small but significant step that confirms how collaboration between businesses and local entities can generate shared value by supporting concrete projects.

For cross-border workers who work in Ticino and reside in Lombardy, this type of initiative can represent a model to follow. Environmental sustainability and social inclusion are values that can be promoted through collaboration between public and private entities. The Il Granello cooperative in Cislago is an example of how it is possible to combine attention to the environment with social inclusion, offering concrete opportunities for people with vulnerabilities.

📊 **Concrete data**: The electric minibus, purchased thanks to a CHF 50,000 grant, has allowed the cooperative to reduce CO2 emissions by approximately 3 tonnes per year. Additionally, the electric vehicle has a 40% lower operating cost compared to a traditional vehicle, with an estimated annual saving of CHF 5,000.

### Practical implications
Using an electric minibus for the cooperative's activities not only reduces environmental impact but also improves the quality of life for the young people involved. Travel becomes easier and more sustainable, allowing participation in more initiatives and workshops. This type of approach can be replicated in other realities, promoting sustainable mobility and social inclusion.

💡 Operational checklist:
- Needs assessment: Analyze the number of people to be transported and the distances covered.
- Funding: Seek public and private contributions, such as the Ticino Canton's sustainable mobility grant (2023).
- Infrastructure: Verify the availability of charging stations in transit municipalities, such as Lugano, Mendrisio, and Chiasso.
- Training: Train staff in the operation of electric vehicles.

### Comparison with the previous situation
Before the arrival of the electric minibus, the Il Granello cooperative in Cislago had to rely on traditional vehicles, with a greater environmental impact. The availability of an electric vehicle represents a significant step forward, not only in terms of sustainability but also efficiency and convenience for the cooperative's users. This example shows how collaborations between businesses and local entities can bring concrete benefits to the community.

📊 Scenario comparison:
- Emissions: A diesel minibus emits approximately 200 grams of CO2 per kilometer, while the electric one emits zero grams.
- Costs: The average cost of an electric minibus is approximately CHF 60,000, compared to CHF 40,000 for a traditional vehicle, but the operational savings compensate for the initial investment in 5-7 years.
- Maintenance: An electric vehicle requires less maintenance, with an estimated annual saving of CHF 2,000.

⚠️ Regulations:
- Ticino Canton: From 2025, all new vehicles purchased with public funds must be zero-emission.
- Lombardy: Tax incentives for the purchase of electric vehicles, with a contribution of up to 8,000 euros.

> "Sustainability is not just an obligation but an opportunity to create shared value" — Director of the Il Granello cooperative in Cislago.

This project demonstrates how innovation can be a driver of inclusion and sustainability, offering a replicable model in other territorial realities.

## Useful tools to protect your net income
To reduce FX leakage, compare [CHF-EUR exchange options](nav:exchange) and [banks for cross-border workers](nav:banks). ### What to Do to Replicate the Initiative
To replicate the electric minibus initiative, you can follow some concrete steps. First, it is important to identify the specific needs of the community and vulnerable individuals. In Ticino, for example, municipalities like Lugano and Bellinzona have already launched similar projects, with positive results in terms of reducing CO2 emissions. Next, you can seek local partners, such as electric vehicle dealers, who can provide support and collaborate to promote sustainability. In Switzerland, the federal contribution for the purchase of electric vehicles is up to 8,000 CHF, a significant incentive to start similar projects.

### Step-by-Step Procedure
1. **Identify** the needs of the community and vulnerable individuals.
 - Conduct a survey among residents to understand their mobility needs.
 - Collaborate with local associations to identify vulnerable individuals who could benefit from the service.

2. Seek local partners, such as electric vehicle dealers, to obtain support.
 - Contact local dealers to get quotes and information on available models.
 - Evaluate funding options, such as federal contributions and European funds.

3. Promote collaboration between businesses and local entities to generate shared value.
 - Organize meetings with stakeholders to discuss collaboration opportunities.
 - Create a communication plan to promote the project and engage the community.

4. Implement the project and monitor results to continuously improve activities.
 - Launch the electric minibus service and monitor results.
 - Use concrete data to assess the environmental and social impact of the project.

### Useful Tools
To explore opportunities for sustainable mobility and social inclusion, you can use the [sustainability calculator](nav:calculator) available on the site. This tool allows you to assess the environmental impact of activities and identify best practices to promote sustainability.
